data.title: John Hancock Diversified Macro Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • The fund incurred losses in Q4 2025 from positions in fixed income, equities, and commodities.
  • Risk assets remained resilient in Q4, although momentum waned as investors balanced supportive policy expectations against a growing list of risks, including fiscal sustainability, inflation pressures, slowing growth, and softer labor markets.
  • Trade policy volatility, elevated deficits, and geopolitical fragmentation have become embedded features of the macro landscape, influencing term premia, cross-asset correlations, and the reliability of historical diversification relationships.

Market review and outlook

The risk-on environment seen in the third quarter extended into Q4, despite continued uncertainty around trade policy, fiscal expansion, and geopolitical developments. October featured leadership changes in France and Japan, continued trade tensions between the United States and China, and an uncertain economic outlook that was further obscured by a lack of data due to the U.S. government shutdown. Equities extended their upward trajectory, supported by strong earnings, expectations for further monetary easing, and sustained enthusiasm around technology and AI. Global fixed income rallied meaningfully as markets absorbed the U.S. Federal Reserve's (Fed's) 25 basis-point rate cut and the prospect of further central bank easing across developed economies.

November began with uncertainty surrounding the U.S. government shutdown, while hawkish Fed commentary created doubts around a December rate cut, weighing on equity and fixed-income markets. When data ultimately arrived mid-month, it pointed to cooling consumption, softer labor-market momentum, and a notable deterioration in consumer sentiment, which renewed rate-cut expectations and led to a rally in global equity and fixed-income markets.

December saw markets finish the year on a cautiously optimistic note. U.S. equity performance was mixed as investors weighed another Fed rate cut and concerns about the sustainability of the AI rally. Non-U.S. equity markets generally outperformed, reflecting more attractive valuations and improved earnings. Front-end rates in the U.S. and Europe remained anchored by stable policy expectations, while long-end yields in the United States and Japan cheapened materially as growth resilience, supply, and normalization risks pushed term premia higher.

Looking ahead, markets in 2026 are likely to remain volatile and path-dependent. Rather than a single dominant macro narrative, investors may face a series of regime shifts driven by the sequencing of policy decisions, data releases, and geopolitical events. In our view, this backdrop favors flexibility, disciplined risk management, and an ability to adapt quickly as conditions evolve. While uncertainty remains elevated, this environment of dispersion and transition has historically created meaningful opportunities for macro-oriented strategies.

Contributors and detractors

Fixed-income positions generated losses in Q4, mainly from long positions in European, Japanese, Canadian, and Australian rates, as well as mixed positions along the U.S. yield curve. The portfolio incurred losses in equities from mixed positions in U.S. and European benchmark indexes, a short position in the FTSE 100 Index, and a long position in the Hang Seng Index. In commodities, mixed positions in energy markets led to modest losses, which were partially offset by gains from long positions in precious metals and copper. The portfolio recorded profits in currencies from a short position in the Japanese yen versus the U.S. dollar, with smaller gains from long positions in the Mexican peso and the euro versus the U.S. dollar.

Portfolio changes

Effective January 2026, we've included additional active, cross-sectional (multi-market) alpha signals in the fund. Whereas the fund previously applied directional signals independently across each of the 55 traded markets, the new alpha signals introduce multi-market "basket" trades that capture cross-market relationships. These additions seek to enhance diversification by broadening the opportunity set to include dispersion, cross-sectional relationships, curve-shape opportunities, and other multi-market dynamics. With this expansion of alpha signals in the fund, we also added 20 rates, equities, and currency markets, bringing the trading universe up from 55 markets to 75 markets. These markets have increased liquidity in recent years and can provide enhanced diversification benefits. Taken together, the expansion of markets and alpha signals is designed to broaden the opportunity set across varied market environments and improve the consistency of outcomes over time.

References

  1. The portfolio managers listed here reflect recent changes to the portfolio team. Please see the prospectus for details.
  2. It is not possible to invest in an index. Index definitions can be found within the "What you should know before Investing" section.
  3. "Net (what you pay)" represents the effect of a contractual fee waiver and/or expense reimbursement and is subject to change.

What you should know before investing

Exposure to commodities and commodities markets may also subject the fund to greater volatility than investments in traditional securities. Commodity investments can be volatile and are affected by speculation, supply-and-demand dynamics, geopolitical stability, and other factors. data.title: Cirrus Aircraft: The Only Pure-Play General Aviation Stock Is A Strong Buy
data.description:
The aerospace and defense industry is a big industry with many companies. However, there are few pure-play companies focused on general aviation. With this report, I am adding the only pure-play company that I could find, which is Cirrus Aircraft (CRRSF), and explaining why I believe the company is a buy.

Cirrus Aircraft Provides Premium Airplanes

Cirrus Aircraft provides premium airplanes, operating a business focused on aircraft owners and high-net-worth individuals. The company has two revenue buckets, which are aircraft manufacturing and services. Aircraft manufacturing is Cirrus’ core business, accounting for 85% of revenues with two main product lines. The first one is the SRX2 Series piston aircraft, and the second one is the Vision Jet. The company differentiates itself with safety systems such as the CAPS parachute and Safe Return Autoland feature.

The second revenue stream is the high-margin aftermarket business providing recurring revenues from maintenance, parts, upgrades, pilot training, aircraft management, digital services, and financing.

Double-Digit Growth on Revenues With a Slight Lift in Margins

In 2025, revenues increased 13.1% to $1.35 billion, with aircraft revenues growing 12.5% to $1.15 billion, driven by a combination of higher volume and mix, while services and other revenues increased 17% to $203 million. Gross profit grew 15.4% to $477.2 million, indicating an 80 basis point lift to the margins to 34.5% despite margin pressure from the G7+ launch. Typically, new aircraft come with lower initial margins, and that was no different for Cirrus, but even with that pressure absorbed, the margins still grew. Operating profit increased 13.1% to $176 million, indicating a margin expansion of 20 basis points to 13% despite higher marketing costs in connection with the G7+ launch. EBITDA grew 13.8% to $213 million, indicating a 10-basis-point margin expansion to 15.7%.

The Growth Drivers and Risks for Cirrus Aircraft

For Cirrus Aircraft, the growth driver is mainly the increase in production, which drives services revenues over the long term and a higher value mix. In 2025, the company increased deliveries to 797 units, and with a backlog of over 1,000 aircraft and continued order momentum, the company has strong multi-year visibility on revenue generation.

Beyond volume growth, product mix provides an additional tailwind. The Vision Jet is priced significantly above the SR2X platform and continues to support revenue growth and margin expansion as production scales further.

In terms of margins, there is not a lot of upside apart from G7+ margins maturing, mix shift, and the services growth boosting the company’s composite margins, but given that aircraft deliveries account for 85% of the revenues currently, the growth in services revenues will only provide incremental upside. Risks include any deterioration in demand, since this remains a cyclical market, as well as higher supply chain costs, as inflation and supply chain bottlenecks have significantly increased costs throughout the industries over the past years.

Cirrus Aircraft Initiated With a Strong Buy Rating

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Cirrus Aircraft trades under the OTC ticker CRRSF. We do note that at times liquidity may be lacking, giving investors a hard time buying or selling at desired quantities and volumes. Cirrus Aircraft’s ordinary shares trade at the Hong Kong Stock Exchange under the ticker 2507, which provides better liquidity.

I believe that Cirrus Aircraft is a strong buy with a $6.57 price target indicating 25% upside. This upside is supported by 13.6% annual sales growth, with EBITDA and free cash flow growing along the same lines. The company’s backlog and positioning as a premium airplane provider in general aviation, along with aftermarket sales opportunities, should provide long-term growth opportunities.

The margins are expected to dip this year, likely driven by the ramp-up of the G7+ program, but after that I do expect the company to see modest margin expansion along with strong free cash flow improvements with solid cash conversion. We also note that the company is in a net cash position, which, combined with positive free cash flows, should allow the company to continue investing in the business and grow shareholder returns.

Conclusion: Cirrus Aircraft Looks Attractive

I believe that Cirrus Aircraft looks attractive. The business is primarily focused on general aviation, so there is a cyclical demand risk, but we also note that the company focuses on the premium segment within the general aviation market and has pioneered safety solutions, while a growing installed base should help long-term growth in services revenues. I believe that the stock is attractively positioned for growth and rate the stock a strong buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: POAHY: Porsche AG Sales Decline, Yet It Is A Positive Signal
data.description:
When I analyze holdings, the first metric I compare is NAV relative to price. And on POAHY today, we are dealing with a discount of almost 35%.

A wide discount, worth taking a closer look at. The market has to deal with a contraction in the margins of the core holdings, which negatively affects expectations for the holding’s dividends. Specifically, attention today goes to Porsche AG, which accounts for about 25% of the POAHY holding.

This is because the Porsche reports U.S. retail sales for first quarter 2026 reported a contraction of more than 12% in total. A figure I had been eager to confirm compared with my last coverage. The question is: do the latest sales numbers really change the thesis on POAHY? My honest opinion?

No.

But before getting into the calculations behind this reasoning…

A TL;DR of the latest analyses

For new readers, I invite you to follow my narrative thread, started here, and continued with this coverage. I still think it is necessary to provide a quick summary of what POAHY is before introducing the thesis:

  • First of all, the holding is not simply composed of VW (53.3%) + Porsche AG (25%+1). There is also a growing "portfolio" segment: AI for trucks (Waabi), mobility (Flix), military drones (Quantum Systems). An element I want to focus on because it clearly shows the holding’s intention to move towards the tech/industrial segment (widely rewarded by the market in recent months). A segment that still does not play a significant role in distributions, therefore in expected returns, but that could in the future.

  • A second element an investor should consider is the U-turn on EV by Porsche. POAHY has stated that ICE and PHEV models will have an extended life, and that some ICE will become "brand-defining". This while the EV market is growing globally (+30% Europe, +43% Germany). The paradox: 60% of Macan sales are already EV, and the declines in 2025 come from ICE, not from electric. They are going against real market data.
  • A large part of the return will depend on the dividend flow because POAHY distributes >80% to shareholders. Therefore, the dividend depends on the flows of VW and Porsche AG (the core holdings). It follows that if margins decline, expected returns of POAHY also fall (regardless of the stock price).

The NAV discount

The analytical core of my last coverage was the discount to NAV, higher than the average and still within the 31–35% range. It means that if POAHY sold its holdings at their fundamental value, a 31% upside would be generated compared to the market price. And this is why I like working on POAHY and, more generally, on holdings, including industrial ones.

A discount on which future market expectations are shaped. Movements in the discount fully express shareholders’ opinion regarding the company’s direction. An opinion that depends on expected returns, which for more than 80% derive from dividend distributions. The dividend engine, however, must now deal with a phase of change in the industrial automotive segment. In the last coverage I explored both the changes in VW and in Porsche AG, still defining the shareholders’ reaction as “reassuring”. In the end, the Supervisory Board itself had explicitly cited "potential decline in electric sales" as a specific risk. And what I focused on is the fact that this did not generate a shock in the discount. Apparently, shareholders appreciate this change in direction. And the question I ask myself is: now that the decline has actually materialized, what makes sense to expect?

Sales

Do the latest sales numbers of Porsche AG really change the thesis on POAHY, or do they confirm that the NAV discount remains justified?

Let’s start from the facts; summarizing the reading of the Porsche U.S. retail sales report, in my opinion these are the most relevant points to keep in mind.

Superficially, one might think: volumes down = weakness. In the end, the US Q1 26 numbers show -12.5%. But in reality, removing the 718 (discontinued, therefore accounting noise), the real organic decline is about -5%. What really weighs is electric: BEVs were supposed to be the revolution, and instead they are struggling while traditional cars (911) are growing organically (+83%).

And this is precisely the answer to the U-turn on EV, demonstrating that it is not just an ideological choice, but that it is producing a rotation of the mix toward very high-margin products. So the right question is not "did they sell less?" but "what did they sell?"

What impact could it have on POAHY?

In the last analysis, I focused on Porsche AG’s operating margin: a drop from 15.7% to 5.5% is not a good thing for POAHY’s expected returns, which depend precisely on the distribution of aggregated profits. The logic was: with high payoffs, the stock risks having to cut dividends, and this would widen the NAV discount (shareholder dissatisfaction). But attention: the Q1 2026 mix actually suggests a possible trajectory of margin recovery, not through volume, but through premium mix. Simply put, the 911 is the product with the highest pricing power and unit margin in Porsche’s portfolio. And it is what Porsche AG’s sales direction seems to be focusing on.

A rotation of the mix toward the 911 and away from the Taycan (-40%) and the 718 (discontinued) has a positive effect on Porsche AG’s margins. Trying to sum it up, 1,740 incremental units (the difference between 911 units sold in Q1 2026 vs Q1 2025) × estimated ASP give an approximate additional revenue. The average ASP of the 911 is around $150,000–$180,000. Therefore, considering 1,740 incremental units × ~$160,000, there would be an additional contribution of ~$280 million. Assuming even just a 20% unit margin, there would be $56 million of additional operating profit. So, limiting the analysis to Porsche AG, the decline in sales, if read as a shift toward more profitable segments, would not negatively affect the margin size in my opinion.

Risk

But attention, POAHY is not a pure play on the 911 or on the Porsche brand. It is a concentrated holding also dependent on other brands, primarily VW. To arrive at an upgrade, it would be necessary to see that the sales data credibly improves upstream cash flow. The potential contribution from Porsche AG’s change in direction would be marginal: considering that POAHY holds only 25% of Porsche. The impact of an additional $56 million is relatively limited (less than 1% mathematically speaking). While POAHY shareholders are interested in an overall contribution that can stimulate the group’s dividend. This is, in my opinion, the key to seeing a further reduction in the NAV discount.

What to expect from VW?

Since VW represents about 53.3% of POAHY’s portfolio, a comparison in this direction is also necessary. We have already covered the 2025 numbers here, and it is important to remember that the adjusted operating margin, which is what matters to POAHY shareholders, stood at 4.6%, or 5.5% excluding tariff effects, with gross cash flow down €6.7 billion year over year (a negative element for POAHY’s dividend engine).

What needs to be done today is to look ahead, to 2026: specifically, I am referring to VW’s 2026 guidance: beyond revenue growth between 0% and 3%, what interests me is the operating margin between 4% and 5.5%, net cash flow between €3 and €6 billion; therefore not a contraction compared to 2025, but rather operational linearity. Extending further to a 3–4 year horizon, which is the timeframe of interest for an investor, it is worth noting how management describes the path toward the 2030 margin target of 8–10% as linear, with no step change, no catalyst. All this without a clear contrarian view on BEV, which instead seems to concern Porsche more directly. But an 8–10% margin would at least make the dividends currently communicated by management sustainable. And this is, in my opinion, a positive element in perspective for POAHY shareholders.

My opinion

Both Porsche AG and VW do not offer compelling numbers when evaluated today. This justifies a wide NAV discount, above the 31–35% range. At the moment, shareholder returns are compressed. But looking forward, in both cases there seems to be a positive linear path over the next 2–3 years. I therefore think that the right rating for POAHY remains HOLD. Based on POAHY and VW data, expected dividends appear sustainable, an element that, in my opinion, supports demand and therefore at least facilitates the maintenance or contraction of the NAV discount.

Conclusion

To conclude, I think that Porsche AG’s U.S. retail sales, although in contraction, are only a confirmation of the company’s change in direction, which within POAHY represents about 25%. Therefore, it does not worsen the contraction of expected margins, already announced by the board, and therefore not even (in my opinion) the NAV-to-price discount (because it does not worsen the size of the expected dividend cut).

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Don't Ignore Brightstar Lottery
data.description:
Recent geopolitical chaos and surging commodities have led to a trying time on the stock market. Some have surged only to turn on a dime when the situation changes, while other stocks have fallen out of favor despite being at most tangentially related to the ongoing situations.

I think this is a good time to start looking for “flight to safety” type of stocks to invest in, but with historical picks like gold miners similarly behaving in unconventional ways, it may be worth thinking outside the box a bit with our analysis.

Which brought me to Brightstar Lottery (BRSL). The London-based company has restructured into being a pure-play lottery system and service provider over the past year. That’s quite a niche, but it also might be one of the more interesting niches given the uncertainty surrounding the direction of the market. Lotteries are not only perceived as recession-proof, but there is also actually some evidence that this particular form of gambling tends to grow consistently in a recessionary environment. Meanwhile, in a bull market, Brightstar could be seen as a very respectable income stock with a strong history of returning value to shareholders. Those facts combined were what inspired me to take a closer look at the company and its potential as a flight-to-safety investment.

From IGT to Brightstar Lottery, Becoming a Pure-Play

This time last year, this company was known as International Game Technology, or IGT. They were lottery-heavy, to be sure, but also had substantial slot machine-related and general digital gambling segments. During the summer, they divested their IGT assets to Apollo Funds in a multi-billion-dollar, all-cash deal and decided to focus entirely on the lottery. They received $4.0 billion in cash, which for a company this size is enormous.

What are they doing with all this cash? They did a mix of value returned directly to shareholders through a special dividend and a stock buyback, while also committing billions toward trying to grow their lottery business, which is now the entirety of their business.

More Lottery by Way of Italy

The potential obstacle to growth was that Brightstar already was a market leader in the world of lottery, so opportunities for growth weren’t necessarily going to be easy to find. Enter Italy.

Italy has a long history of being very interested in lotteries, and their SuperEnalotto is arguably the world’s single biggest lottery drawing. It’s a big market, and while Brightstar already had an important presence in Italy through its subsidiary LottoItalia, they decided that was the place to invest in growth.

In November, LottoItalia was awarded a 9-year agreement to operate the Italy Lotto. Italy Lotto is a competitor of SuperEnalotto within Italy and a growing one that tries to attract customers with better odds. The upfront license fee on this deal cost them 2.2 billion euros ($2.58 billion), so that’s clearly the big use of this $4.0 billion cash influx. The growth of Italy Lotto is the major opportunity for Brightstar to start meaningfully growing revenue after years of relatively flat results.

2023

2024

2025

United States

Italy

$1.02 billion

Rest of Europe

Other

$146 million

$123 million

(Source: most recent 20-F from the SEC)

Italy was already Brightstar’s one source of growth, and the heavy investment in Italy Lotto is an attempt to both continue and extend those gains. Will it ultimately be worth the $2.58 billion upfront costs? The Italian concession wasn’t cheap, but it’s higher quality than most of the international ones, so the company has reason to believe it was worthwhile.

Italy isn’t just growing; it’s the only thing that’s been growing in recent years, so doubling down on it makes sense. But does Brightstar as a whole make sense at these numbers?

Modest Earnings, but Potential for Growth

2023

2024

2025

Total Revenue

$2.53 billion

$2.51 billion

$2.51 billion

Attributable Operating Income

$116 million

($1 million)

Attributable Net Income

GAAP EPS

78¢

Non-GAAP EPS

67¢

91¢

(Source: most recent 20-F from the SEC)

You can see where Brightstar’s top line has been decidedly consistent for years. 2025 was a transition year, both because of their restructuring and because of a foreign exchange loss that weakened operating results, though it ultimately came in with base-line a pretty standard bottom line result.

The GAAP P/E ratio is 16.55, which is roughly in line with the sector’s 16.69. Non-GAAP P/E numbers are 13.46, which is somewhat lower than the sector median of 15.49. By these metrics, Brightstar looks mostly priced in line with what you'd expect out of the sector. To justify it as an investment, we're going to need something that puts them ahead of the pack, and in this case that means dividends.

Returning Value: The Growing Dividend

We mentioned the special dividend before, but since that’s already been paid, that shouldn’t factor substantially into the estimate of Brightstar as an investment. That money’s gone and paid, though it does establish the company’s mentality as one of returning value to shareholders, and that shouldn’t be underestimated. Not every company is as laser-focused on directly rewarding the shareholder as Brightstar's recent behavior shows they are.

So what about the regular dividend? That's the real news here, as it's large and growing. After a decade of paying 20¢ per quarter, Brightstar this year increased the payout to 23¢ per quarter. That'd be an above-market, growing dividend in the best of times for the stock, but after recent sell-offs it's the aspect that puts the company head-and-shoulders above alternatives in the sector.

23¢ quarterly amounts to a 7.51% yield at current prices, which is wildly better than the sector median of 2.46%. Brightstar's valuation on a purely P/E basis may not make them stand out, but as an income stock, it’s shaping up to be a real winner. Strong cash flow suggests this level is readily sustainable.

2023

2024

2025

Operating Cash Flow

$1.01 billion

$1.05 billion

($99 million)

CapEx

($147 million)

($149 million)

($316 million)

Net Investing Cash Flow

($393 million)

($357 million)

$3.5 billion

Dividends Paid

($160 million)

($161 million)

($770 million)

(Source: most recent 20-F from the SEC)

Keeping in mind 2025 is an outlier on multiple fronts because of the sale of the casino segment and the special dividend payment, the underlying point here is that, notwithstanding one-time payments made to Italy for the aforementioned contract, operating cash flow has tended to be a hair above $1 billion annually. CapEx eats some of that, but even the increased regular dividend at 23¢ per quarter only amounts to about $172 million annually, which is easily affordable here. At these prices, finding a stock with that high of a yield and one that's so easily covered by its operations makes Brightstar particularly appealing. But what about the rest of the cash generated above and beyond the dividend?

That's where share repurchases come in. Last year, Brightstar bought back around 16.2 million, spending around half of the authorized $500 million repurchase. More buybacks are to be expected, and in February’s conference call, they reported another 2.1 million were bought back in early 2026. Since the stock price has fallen in recent months, Brightstar should be getting more bang for their buck with this year’s portion of the buyback. Eliminating shares through the buyback only makes the dividend more sustainable going forward, and in my view, raises the possibility of increasing it further in the future. That's particularly true if the company's strategy gets them back to meaningful revenue growth.

A Lot Depends on Italy

Perhaps it goes without saying, but investing €2.2 billion both creates an asset and necessarily a risk. Brightstar can no longer afford to view Italy as a side business. This investment means Italy absolutely needs to remain a winner for them and needs to continue growing and generating money for them. If Italy doesn’t grow enough to justify their big expense, Brightstar’s management could look very foolish for bidding so hard on the Italy Lotto concession.

The company is similarly trying to grow its business online through what it calls iLottery. While this remains less significant than the physical business in the United States and Italy, it could be another cash sink for the company in that we don’t really know it will ultimately generate revenue commensurate with the investment being made in it. The potential is there, so be sure, but in breaking new ground in the business, they will also run into unforeseeable new challenges.

Brightstar wanted to be a pure-play lottery company, and that means any challenges related to their lottery business will reflect more directly on the company. Not diversifying across multiple markets confers risk, even if the lottery is seemingly a relatively stable generator of cash.

The Price is Right, the Dividend is Better

While the company's restructuring as a pure-play lottery provider is a work in progress, I’m rating Brightstar as a buy, especially after the stock fell to new 52-week lows in recent weeks. The earnings valuation is in line with expectations, but the high and readily supportable dividend yield really makes this stock shine at these prices, particularly in an uncertain market. As mentioned before, lotteries are historically resilient even in difficult economic times, which we may or may not be heading into.

Investors should keep a close eye on Italy Lotto, because clearly as that goes, so goes Brightstar. So long as the cash flow is enough to sustain the dividend, though, which right now it plainly is, the stock seems substantially undervalued.

I think Brightstar Lottery is ultimately an unconventional flight to safety stock, and its yield makes it a promising contrarian play at a time when opportunities for safety are few and far between.

data.title: Polaris: Not Attractive Enough Yet
data.description:
The savvy and valuation-conscious investor with a fair bit of luck could make a case that my thesis on Polaris, given in the article found here, was ill-timed and that profit could have been made by investing in Polaris (PII) contrary to my forecast. While this would be true, the return that I believe most investors are likely to have as a result of the company is, for the time being, negative, as per the following RoR and underperformance.

Polaris is a trickier business than investors give it credit for. Many analysts cite Polaris as being a safe and reliable manufacturer of off-road vehicles, even with military and other appealing applications. They, however, miss that the company, in the past few years, has been tested on exactly these assumptions, and in many cases failed to live up to its expectations - and the proof of this is both in results and other analyzable factors in the companies.

Whenever something is coined as being a "value investment", I tend to pay attention. Many times, what I am seeing is, in fact, a value investment, an attractive one, that I can invest in. Other times, however, what we turn out to have is an investment that, for one reason or another, is in fact not a value investment at all, but a mix of a value trap and a company that has seen its "best days" come and go.

The two sides for the company we're looking at are basically one saying that this is a value investment, with recent results reinforcing the value proposition, and the other, to which I belong, saying that this is not necessarily a value investment and needs to be priced contrary to how it used to be viewed a few years ago.

In this article, I will look at my thesis given 4 months ago, what signs there are that I should upgrade my $40 PT that I gave for the company in that piece, and what (if any) signs there are that things are not looking up at all.

Polaris - Upside is There if You Premiumize or if It Turns Around

The idea that Polaris is an investment that has trickier upside and fundamentals than investors seem willing to give it is hardly a new one, nor one that's underrepresented by the market today. Both Wall Street and Quant have the company at a conservative "HOLD" rating, with the SA average being "BUY" currently, but there are also indicators in other articles and ratings that things are not, in fact, looking up.

The often-cited issue is growth - as in there is very little or none at all. In addressing the fiscal 2025 period, we find that Polaris reported sales of about $7B+. Far from being a growth number, this was actually flat compared to previous year results.

Why is this such a problem?

Because we're in a high inflation environment, by most metrics. In a high inflation environment, a flat revenue number does not actually imply "flat", it implies a decline. In an inflationary environment, I would argue that Polaris needed to grow 3-6% just to keep pace. It did not. Growth stalls in high inflation mean (or can mean) that the end market for the company's products, in this case recreational vehicles (specialized) and specialized automotives, is difficult.

When I build a more conservative, or bearish-considered, thesis, I look for signs that are objectively valid for that being the case. On a high level, many of the company's volumes saw a decline. RV sales are down 10% in 2025, for instance. But there are also signs that Polaris is struggling to increase its sales, despite what can only be described as significant promotions. In 2025, the company's gross margins were under pressure by what analysts reported as being higher promotional activity in order for dealers to move inventory. This is never a good sign. Despite shipment volumes being up, the net pricing (meaning what money the company gets to keep per sold vehicle) is down for many quarters now.

This tells me that Polaris is actually prioritizing not profitable sales but sales volumes. When a company is prioritizing volumes over profit, that's dangerous - and I see no indications in the recent results that this is actually changing in a meaningful way - the other way around, actually.

Reduction in dealer inventory levels is now down, but this is a result of the pressure mentioned above - powersports, which is the industry that Polaris is in, continues to be down.

Furthermore, in 2026, when Polaris reported its numbers, management cited very conservative estimates. Rather than expecting significant growth in the top line, the company now expects only 1-3% for the year. That means that 2026 is not going to be in any way a "turnaround" year for Polaris.

The structural decline in demand for the company's products can also be viewed in the context of asset closures. Polaris has quite recently closed its Wisconsin plant, and this speaks in favor of a leaner, more realistic Polaris - which is a good thing—but it works against the assumption that this is only a "transition" in the industry and more in favor of a new reality.

A good exercise is asking what would be needed for the industry to see a complete turnaround. As in, a full recovery to where the company used to be.

This is just my view, of course, but in order for that to materialize, I would need to see lower interest rates. I consider that unlikely. Lower interest rates would be important because they would motivate consumers to spend money on what I consider to be high-priced discretionary items, like this. But again, both macro and consumer credit standards (tighter than before) actually speak against this happening.

Polaris specifically also needs to reduce its reliance on external materials and inputs. The company is already doing this by planning to reduce China exposure, but the question is whether it's enough.

So, Polaris is, from some perspectives, doing what it can to address some of these issues. However, for Polaris to really get past and move into a new reality, I believe the company needs to take another step forward. I believe Polaris needs to move from a "push" to a "pull" model. What this means is that part of the reason for the volatility was the ZIRP and pandemic boom, which clouded inventory levels and demands. It needs to move to a model where it only produces what is already selling or is sold. This means a few things. It likely means that volatility is going to be less than before, but lower valuations are due to more realistic, but lower, sales numbers. But it also enables the company to move away from strategies that require it to promote old stock at margin-destroying levels, which not only hurts the bottom line but, more importantly, destroys the premium image of the brand. It's the same thing that I argue happened with BMW (BMWKY) and Mercedes when they introduced substantial financing options and lower-end entry cars. It may have increased sales, and I'm favorable at least on BMW, but it also killed at least a part of the view that the brands are "premium, above others".

A brand like Polaris needs to be very careful not only in what it does but also in how what it does impacts its brand. This is not helped by the quality issues that have now been a part of its story for several years.

To justify a higher valuation, Polaris needs to do, in my view, many of these things (some the company is already doing), and then I'd be willing to once again consider it a "BUY" at a more "reasonable" level. Remember, I used to own stock in the business, but I got out between 2021 and 2022.

What does the current valuation tell us?

Valuation for Polaris - Upside Requires More Clarity; I'm Not Moving My PT

Not much that is interesting to me, unfortunately. The current analyst estimates are inflated insofar as they mostly seem to be in favor of a complete reversal of the company, with a 127% annualized EPS growth rate based on 17-18 analysts.

While I concur that "a" recovery will happen, I remain very skeptical as to the exact nature and level of this recovery and what this will mean for the valuation. The expectations above are based on a 90-100x P/E, which I consider to be completely speculative and unrealistic.

If we estimate the company at a 20-year average of about 16x P/E, we arrive at a price of around $65/share, similar to the targets I cited in my last article, found here.

The problem is that right now the company trades at $55/share, and despite an AEPS that doesn't cover the dividend for 2025, it is unlikely to cover it for 2026, and the upside, even with the close to 5% yield toward that target, is less than 13% per year.

So even in the case of a normalization to a 20-year average, to an AEPS that rises to more than 200% of the currently expected 2026E $1.65/share by 2028E, even under those assumptions, Polaris is not expected to outperform my hurdle rate of 15% per year. Furthermore, the company's historical accuracy does not speak in favor of these levels even occurring, and based on the risks I cited above, I would consider them even less realistic than that.

My estimate of about $40/share in PT may be considered excessively low, but it's also a PT that actually shows, if it drops that far, a considerable and, above all, realistic upside to a more conservative recovery. Given all of the challenges that the company faces, I would avoid being blinded by what I see as a company facing a new reality (not a transition), and therefore I would consider Polaris to be a "HOLD" here, and my $40/share PT to be unchanged and still valid after 4 months' time.

Risks & Considerations

I continue to believe in 2026 that PII has more risks than most shareholders seem to be currently willing to give the company. The series of failures and recalls has, I believe, dented the customer's faith in the company, and this remains a headwind. While the company has a very solid market position, Ducati, BRP (DOO), CFMOTO, and Kubota (KUBTY) have alternatives for many of the company's segments, and some of those are also valued more attractively.

All in all, even if the company were to trade at historical multiples and we had the 2027E EPS in this year, I still believe the resulting P/E would be too high for the company in its current state, and I see no reason for me to change my assumptions on this one.

Thesis

Polaris was once a company I invested in and thought well of. Following tariff and macro impacts, as well as a series of recalls, and mechanical and quality issues with the company's products, it's unclear to me where the company is going from here, and the picture remains clouded in 2026. While it's technically a qualitative business (provided it manages to turn things around, at least), I would be careful about investing here, and I would be even more careful about investing at a high valuation.

A low price is a must for me for this company. I believe anything above $40/share PT to be illusory for the longer term. Thus, I consider the company to be a "HOLD" here and not worthy of a conservative investment. This makes me one of the analysts with the lowest PTs, but given the trends, that's something I'm very comfortable with. The dividend is not well covered now or likely for 2026, based on current assumptions.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is qualitative
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills 2 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 2/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this and a price target of $40/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of April 2026.

data.title: Why Adobe Is A 'Strong Buy' Despite The AI Boogeyman
data.description:
Adobe (ADBE) is still hanging in there even if the stock price might imply otherwise. AI continues to improve rapidly, increasing investor concern for the future outlook. ADBE faces considerable risk for seat-based revenues, but investors may be underestimating the collective defense from robust GAAP profitability, share repurchases, and potential for AI to pose benefits to compensate for the risks. I reiterate my strong buy rating for the stock.

ADBE Stock Price

I last covered ADBE in February where I rated the stock a strong buy given my view that the bearish outlook and sentiment for software stocks was more than offset by the deeply discounted valuation. The stock has fallen 13% since.

The visible catalyst might feel indirect, but I expect incumbent resilience and financial strength to prevail in the end.

ADBE Stock Key Metrics

ADBE is a software company with deep integrations across creative and marketing applications. The company has integrated AI across its product suite including with its popular Firefly product. That however has not been enough to shake the AI boogeyman fears as investors appear concerned that adding AI to existing legacy products might not be enough to compete against newer competitors that are built from AI from the very beginning.

Nonetheless, the company has seen rapid adoption of its AI products, with Firefly seeing 45% QoQ consumption growth and Firefly Enterprise seeing customers growing 50% YoY.

While the stock appears to be pricing in pressures to the long term terminal value, the company is still growing. Revenues jumped 12% YoY to $6.4 billion, exceeding guidance of between $6.25 billion to $6.3 billion in revenue. The company generated $6.06 in non-GAAP earnings per share, beating guidance of $5.90 per share.

Management also guided the company to see solid results moving forward, with the second quarter expected to see up to $6.48 billion in revenue, representing 10.3% YoY growth. The market worries that this is a melting ice cube, but melting ice cubes don’t typically grow their top-lines at a double-digit clip.

Management expects the company to generate around $26.1 billion in revenue for the full year, representing 9.7% YoY growth. CEO Shantanu Narayen notably is set to step down from the CEO role after 18 years at the helm. I, as I suspect many others, interpreted this somewhat negatively as this is certainly not the most ideal circumstances to leave the post. Even so, sentiment has dominated the trading action and I expect actual numbers to still be the most important drivers over the long term.

On the conference call, management appeared confident in their business, noting that investors should “be assured that we're spending on the newer initiatives.” That said, they did note some challenges, such as that their “traditional stock business saw a steeper decline than we expected,” but their “focus remains on giving customers meaningful choice between stock and generative AI.” I expect investors to be looking for any signs of demand deterioration, even if the current numbers suggest anything but. Management noted that the headwinds in the stock business pressured growth rates by roughly 30 bps. Management noted that they “don't see any reason why the trends we've seen in the business historically would inflect to drive a different dynamic” but of course investors have clearly voted with their dollars that this time is different.

Is ADBE Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

ADBE had seen its valuation compress due to AI fears and absolutely flatten out following the release of Claude Mythos. These feel more significant in nature than OpenAI discontinuing their video generation app Sora.

At recent prices, ADBE traded at just around 3.5x sales.

That has put the stock at a high-single-digit multiple of earnings.

I have faced a rude awakening with regards to AI disruption risks and squarely view estimates as being too optimistic. Given ADBE’s high exposure to seat-based pricing as well as AI’s likely “good enough” offerings for the lower-end yet important segment, I find it more likely that top-line growth slows to the mid-single-digit range as soon as next year, if not lower. Veteran investors may be wary of the risks of investing in stocks amidst a likely future revision lower in estimates yet here I am sticking with a “strong buy” rating. I am not going to attempt to defend any view that ADBE might see growth re-accelerate from here, I no longer see that as likely even as ADBE integrates more AI product offerings. But I expect revenue to be resilient as I expect larger and existing customers to face significant inertia in switching away to competing products. That doesn’t mean the company is completely out of the woods. The real risk may be pressure to the seat based pricing model as AI allows its customers to replace human labor with AI agents. It is extremely difficult to predict to what extent and how quickly such pressures might materialize, but the key point is that ADBE would be able to benefit from such cost reductions itself - after all gross margins remain very high with fixed costs representing the vast majority of the cost structure. I expect ADBE to be able to offset pressures from AI with this resiliency and AI-driven margin expansion. In contrast with my prior coverage, I no longer see as much multiple expansion upside as the growth engine is no longer as reliable as before. That said, the stock is still too cheap here and appears to offer a solid risk-reward profile given the reasonably leveraged balance sheet, robust profitability, and resilient business (albeit disappointment in growth). I can see the stock delivering around 15% annual returns moving forward, driven by the earnings yield, modest earnings growth, and minimal implied multiple expansion.

ADBE Stock Risks

It is possible that ADBE is outright disrupted by AI. Perhaps it is unable to hold on to legacy business as expected. This may lead to declines in both revenues and profits. Perhaps the company is unable to offset AI pressures with AI-driven efficiencies of its own. Perhaps competitors are able to take market share in dramatic fashion, helped by AI. Management might conduct expensive M&A in attempts to reignite growth, at the cost of shareholder dilution or pressures to the balance sheet.

ADBE Stock Conclusion

ADBE is still posting strong numbers and I expect it to do so at least in the near term. I now expect significant volatility within a matter of quarters as AI is likely to have a disruptive impact for the lower-end market. I however expect ADBE to benefit from the very risks it faces. ADBE is a core holding in the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my string buy rating for the stock.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 04/13/26
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: Macy's: Healthy 4% Dividend Amid Bump In Comp Sales
data.description:
With the stock market choppy and short term interest rates continuing to fade throughout 2026, many investors have increasingly turned to dividend stocks to boost their income portfolios this year. And yet many dividend trades, such as energy stocks that have benefited from soaring oil prices, have become crowded and expensive.

Macy’s (M), in my view, is an excellent combination of yield, value, and an operational turnaround. While it’s true that the company faces headwinds from a tough macroeconomy, there are green shoots in this classic retailer as it achieves positive comp sales in a tough retail landscape. With shares of Macy’s down ~15% since the start of the year, we have a compelling opportunity to buy this stock on the dip.

I last wrote a buy article on Macy’s in January, when the stock was trading at $21 per share. Since then, Macy’s has fallen ~10%, which to me only accentuates the value appeal of this stock. Holiday sales emerged stronger than expected, and I believe Macy’s current outlook for the current year to be quite conservative given recent trends. I’m reiterating my buy rating here.

As a reminder for investors who are newer to Macy’s, here are what I consider to be the top reasons to be bullish on the stock in the long haul:

  • Driving improvement in comp sales via a business shift toward higher income consumers. The “Bold New Chapter” initiative is focusing on shuttering underperforming stores and upgrading existing ones with better lighting, more curated merchandising, and higher staffing levels. These initiatives are aimed at transforming Macy’s into a higher-end retailer with a more premium in-store shopping experience. So far, positive sales comps suggest this strategy is working.
  • Focused on efficiency rather than growth at all costs. Macy’s ended FY25 with 665 stores, shuttering 15 net locations in the year. Its focus on shrinking its store portfolio to the best locations is allowing the company to preserve its profitability.
  • Managing tariffs well. Gross margins are declining by less than 1 point y/y, with stronger merchandising and product margin helping to offset tariff burdens. There’s opportunity if tariffs end up getting reversed.
  • Healthy payout ratios. The company spent less than one-quarter of its FY25 FCF on dividends.

Stay long here: in my view, Macy’s remains an appealing value play.

Q4 recap

Let’s now go through Macy’s latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Macy’s revenue declined -1.7% y/y to $7.64 billion, which handily beat Wall Street’s expectations y $7.51 billion (-3.3% y/y) by nearly a two point margin.

We note that the revenue declines are principally a function of the company’s intentional, targeted store closures in order to remove underperforming locations and elevate the Macy’s brand. Amid a tough holiday quarter, we note that each of Macy’s core “nameplates” achieved positive comp sales, which excludes the headwind from store closures.

Macy’s achieved 0.4% comp sales growth (versus 2% in Q3; on a go-forward only including sustaining stores, comps were slightly stronger at 0.6%), while Bloomingdale’s was the star performer at 9.9% comps growth (accelerating versus 9% in Q3). Bluemercury, the company’s skincare and fragrances arm, achieved 1.6% comps growth, also accelerating 50bps versus 1.1% growth in Q3.

Management continues to highlight the success at bringing customers back to Macy’s reimagined stores through new brand launches and more curated in-store experiences. Per CEO Antony Spring’s remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

Macy's nameplate achieved 0.6% go-forward comparable sales growth, representing a 190 basis point improvement versus last year and a 690 basis point improvement on a 2-year basis. Performance was led by the Reimagine 125 locations in digital. The Reimagine 125 comparable sales grew 1%. Stores that have received initiatives delivered positive comps in 7 of the past 8 quarters and serve as a strong proof point of our ability to return to growth. Performance reflects our customer-led focus, including clear and purposeful changes at Macy's. We've improved our assortment relevancy.

We're investing in staffing and events, and we're leveraging local market strengths. These initiatives are having a halo effect on Macy's omnichannel business and contributing to positive digital comparable sales results. Speaking of digital, Macy's digital channel represents approximately 1/3 of our annual sales. It is benefiting from a modernized Macy's- Official Site inspired by what we're doing in stores. Over the past year, we have shifted to a more editorialized approach that reinforces our fashion authority and facilitate shopping across categories that drives commerce”.

On the profitability side, Macy’s gross margins declined only -50bps y/y to 35.2%, reflecting a 60bps headwind from tariffs (without which the company actually would have seen gross margin expansion). Meanwhile, core adjusted EBITDA held steady even amid a slight boost to sales and marketing spending, declining -3% y/y to $837 million at a 10.6% margin (-20bps y/y). Pro forma EPS of $1.67 beat Wall Street consensus of $1.55 with 8% upside.

Healthy dividend coverage and cheap valuation against FY26 outlook

Let’s now shift into the crux of my buy thesis on Macy’s: the stock trades cheaply relative to its improving fundamentals, while investors also have a relatively safe dividend to bank on.

At current share prices near $19, Macy’s trades at a market cap of $4.97 billion. Netting off the $1.25 billion of cash and $2.43 billion of debt on Macy’s balance sheet (which as a reminder is quite long-dated, with the majority of maturities happening post-2032), the company’s resulting enterprise value is $6.15 billion.

For FY26, Macy’s has guided to $21.40-$21.65 billion in revenue, which reflects -2% to -1% overall decline, on a ~flat range for comparable sales (excluding the impact of store closures and net new openings). Meanwhile, the company expects 7.7-7.9% adjusted EBITDA margins against this revenue profile, indicating $1.68 billion in adjusted EBITDA at the midpoint.

This positions Macy’s valuation multiples at:

  • 3.7x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA
  • 9.5x FY26 P/E

We note that Macy’s multiples sit well below many retail peers:

Meanwhile, the stock’s recently boosted dividend offers extra incentive to be patient for a potential upward re-rating in multiples. The company recently boosted its quarterly dividend by 5% to 19.15 cents per share, or $0.766 per year (a 4.0% yield at today’s share prices).

The company doesn’t guide to cash flow, but in FY25, Macy’s generated $1.43 billion in operating cash flow and $1.84 billion in adjusted EBITDA, or 78% conversion from EBITDA to OCF. Assuming similar conversion in FY26, guidance midpoint EBITDA of $1.68 billion would yield $1.31 billion OCF in FY26. Taking off $800 million in planned capex leaves $531 million in free cash flow.

The company’s $0.766 annual dividend represents a $201 million annual dividend expense, using 263.0 million shares outstanding as of the end of Q4. This reflects a relatively modest and sustainable 38% payout ratio.

Risks and key takeaways

Of course, there are risks that we need to keep in mind here. Right now Macy’s is focused on efficiency and closing underperforming stores, so we can give it a pass on current revenue declines - but eventually the company will need to showcase a path to growth. Also keep in mind that many retailers have called out a more promotional retail environment. Macy’s could be forced to keep up with discounts and sales in order to maintain market share, which may deepen its margin losses this year.

Regardless, I see more positives than negatives in this stock especially at single digit earnings multiples. Buy for the yield and stay patient through a re-rating

data.title: EVT: Market Pullback And Discount Present Another Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

The Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund (EVT) provides investors with a diversified portfolio of equity investments in a closed-end fund wrapper. The tilt is primarily toward more value-oriented investments, with financials, healthcare, and industrials often making up the largest sector allocations of the underlying portfolio. With the latest market volatility, we've been seeing added pressure on this fund from its leveraged approach. However, the fund going to an even wider discount from our prior update is also adding further pressure.

EVT Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: -1.25
  • Discount/Premium: -9.17%
  • Distribution Yield: 8.31%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.11%
  • Leverage: 18.1%
  • Managed Assets: $2.399 billion
  • Structure: Perpetual

EVT's investment objective is to "provide a high level of after-tax total return consisting primarily of tax-advantaged dividend income and capital appreciation."

They highlight that the fund will invest primarily in "dividend-paying common and preferred stocks and seeks to distribute a high level of dividend income that qualifies for favorable federal income tax treatment."

Thus, the "tax-advantaged" portion of its name, and as for the 2025 distribution classification, this has been achieved. The entire distribution was either qualified dividends or long-term capital gains. Both are relatively tax-friendly when compared to ordinary income rates for most investors.

The fund is leveraged, and that is always worth considering, as it will increase overall volatility and risk for investors. It also comes with added leverage costs, pushing the fund's total expense ratio up to 2.27%.

While the Fed was easing their benchmark rate, we are now in a period where inflation is expected to remain sticky or rise on the back of increased oil prices. Therefore, most investors are anticipating no cuts for this year—and even murmurs of possibly having to increase once again could be on the table.

Performance - Discount Remains Appealing

While the fund has not escaped the latest equity pressures brought on by the conflict in Iran, the fund has still provided some positive total returns since our prior write-up. During this time, the S&P 500 Index has been about flat, though that is the result of this latest equity downturn.

I continue to view EVT as an attractive fund to consider given its discount. In fact, the discount has moved slightly wider during this period as well. That would have pressured the fund's total returns even further relative to the actual underlying portfolio's results. Given that the discount/premium can add additional volatility, which can be seen as a risk, but for other investors, it can present opportunities.

At this current time, the fund's discount is quite wide relative to its longer-term average level of the last decade. On the other hand, this wider discount has been fairly persistent now for the last several years. This can be a sign that this is the new normal level—which wouldn't make it look like an overly large bargain currently.

That said, my argument would be that an even wider discount is relatively rare aside from severe market downturns. Thus, it is still presented as an attractive time to consider adding to one's position.

With the fund being a more value-oriented tilt, they benchmark against the Russell 1000 Value Index. They also include the ICE BofA Fixed Rate Preferred Securities Index. Then they also mix those two indexes together for a blended index benchmark when they provide performance comparisons. Historically, the fund has provided competitive results against the Russell 1000 Value Index directly, and then, when blended, has outperformed.

This is what we should expect to see during good times, with the equity exposure helping to boost the overall results relative to the preferred sleeve. Additionally, the fund is leveraged, so during periods of strong results, that is going to get boosted even further.

Distribution - Monthly Pay

The distribution rate for EVT comes in at 8.31%, and on a NAV basis, that rate works out to 7.54%.

Given the cost of leverage for EVT isn't expected to decline at the moment, net investment income isn't likely to see that added boost of easing interest rates. At the same time, the bulk of the distribution will be covered via capital gains in the underlying portfolio anyway. That has almost always been the case for this fund, and the numbers reflect that of the last five years. So while NII can be the more predictable portion of distribution coverage, it isn't the main component.

Of course, that also then brings up the idea that if we remain in an extended or deepening downturn for the equity market, that will pressure them to potentially have to cut. As we saw in the distribution chart above, that does occur from time to time, needing to make an adjustment. Fortunately, most of the adjustments have been increases. That said, during periods such as the Global Financial Crisis or the bear market of 2022, they had ended up reducing the payout.

EVT's Portfolio

The broader breakdown of the fund's portfolio shows ~80% in equities and the remainder in fixed-income/preferred offerings. The fund is also positioned overwhelmingly in U.S. holdings, accounting for ~93% of the fund. This is consistent with what we've seen regularly with this fund—so no big surprises on that front.

Additionally, the fund has regularly provided overweight allocation toward financials. That's made it one of the diversifier funds for investors that may be otherwise feeling like they are exposed to too much technology weightings. This can help bring them some balance in a diversified way, thanks to its 75 total holdings and heavy financial weighting.

In fact, if we are getting technical, the financial weighting has moved up nearly another point since our last update. Industrials now comes in second place ahead of healthcare, which previously had 12.31% and 14.01% weightings, respectively. Meaning we've seen a decrease in healthcare exposure by nearly 5 points, which was partially offset by technology climbing up from the ~6% weighting before.

In the grand scheme of things, these are some meaningful sector shifts, but over a 9-month period that we are looking at between holdings, this can come from natural market price shifts. Though given the fund's 45% portfolio turnover rate, they were relatively active during this last fiscal year. That could be compared with the average turnover rate they had of 34.8% in the last five years.

In terms of the top holdings, we have seen JPMorgan (JPM) dethroned from its top spot, with Micron Technology (MU) climbing by its percentage weighting.

MU was not a top holding when we last looked at this fund; however, it was a holding listed in the fund around the same time. Looking at the N-PORT filed in September 2025 for holdings as of July 31, 2025, MU was around a 1.5% allocation in the fund. This is one of those names where some market price 'shifting' resulted in changes for the portfolio.

Primarily, that would be in the technology sector, and this was not just any market price shift but a surge in the stock's price. Even with the stock falling into bear territory as it is over 24% down from its 52-week high, the stock is still up nearly 230% since the end of July. This would be thanks to the shortage of memory chips on the back of aggressive AI infrastructure buildout.

Analysts expect this to help boost profits massively for MU over the next handful of years, followed by a relative plunge. On the other hand, even heading into 2028/2029 earnings plunge from estimated 2027 highs, those are still earnings well ahead of what was delivered in fiscal 2025.

Of course, whether these results are achieved or not, that will be the next huge question, as analyst estimates are merely that—estimates. What it has done for EVT, though, is provide some significant unrealized appreciation. At least for now, that is the sort of capital gain source that they can use to fund the monthly payout to investors.

Conclusion

EVT remains a discounted closed-end fund that is worth paying attention to based on an attractive valuation. Given the broader equity downturn, which has presented another added layer of enticement for considering a position. The fund has a long history of paying out monthly distributions to investors, going back to its inception in 2003. While they have had to make some adjustments over time, the monthly payout has been pretty predictable. During good market times, expect the distribution to remain stable or rise. During market downturns, if the distribution rate starts pushing near 10%, then one can consider that a lower distribution might be in the cards.

data.title: Papa John's: A Slice Of Dividend Remains Attractive And Sustainable
data.description:
In recent years, we have seen how vulnerable many fast-food restaurants can be to macroeconomic volatility. And even an established pizza chain like Papa John's International, Inc. (PZZA) is not entirely insulated as inflation continues to squeeze purchasing power and pricing flexibility. The challenge persists until today. That is why it's not surprising that its value still weakened by 1.44% from my previous coverage. Even so, we still can see its resilience as its efforts to stabilize its operations remain effective. Fundamentals promise sustainability and longevity. Valuation and technicals adhere to it as buying opportunities emerge again, although upside potential is not that much.

Papa John's: Some Resilience Amid Mounting Headwinds

The quick-service restaurants are facing multiple challenges amid macroeconomic volatility and stiff competition. These aspects continue to squeeze their pricing flexibility, sales growth, and margins. Even a popular pizza chain like Papa John's International, Inc. is not immune to these headwinds as the impact affects its performance. Yet, its prudent restaurant continues to help improve operational efficiency to stabilize cost pressures. This was visible in its most recent performance.

In Q4 2025, its operating revenue amounted to $498.2M, down by 6.1% YoY from $530.8M. This was its weakest YoY revenue increase/decrease in four quarters. It is also worth noting that the Q4 revenue in the past two years has decreased continuously. This may suggest that the impact of softer market dynamics has intensified. And this was not surprising due to its weakness in the US market. If we look at its performance based on geographical diversification, its domestic operations remained hammered.

On a lighter note, its franchised royalties and fees still increased slightly. Additionally, international operations remained its cornerstone as sales grew by 8.1% YoY, which partially offset the weakness in the other segments. It still appeared manageable as a whole because the global systemwide sales remained flat at $1.23B. This proved that its international expansion remained helpful.

Likewise, its operating costs and expenses decreased. I believe this was impressive even after the increase in its restaurant count amid the rising prices. This may be due to its prudent and well-balanced split between running company-owned restaurants and franchising. While it was not enough to offset the decrease in revenue, it helped PZZA maintain positive profits. In the end, its operating margin remained decent at 4.9% despite its decrease from 5.6% YoY.

Possible Risks To Investors

Mounting inflationary headwinds remain a problem as they continue to affect purchasing power and discretionary spending. Although it has slightly slowed down recently, it remains above the 2.0% Fed target. So, we may infer that inflation is still not under control yet.

Its lagging performance in its domestic and North American markets may remain a challenge for PZZA. Although the number of restaurants increased to 3,523 versus 3,514, systemwide sales in the region remained weak. This can imply two things. One is that customer foot traffic in PZZA store chains there continued to decrease. Second is that it continued to lower its pricing to attract volume. But whatever the reason is, investors can't deny the challenges it faces in the region.

Domino's Pizza (DPZ) is also a primary challenge for PZZA. It continues to dominate the fast-food-style pizza market with its vast global market presence. On top of that, its strong delivery arm and high investment in technologies continue to challenge its peers. Its sustained revenue growth shows that it continues to gain market share relative to peers like PZZA and even Pizza Hut (YUM).

Risk Mitigants

Despite these challenges, I believe that PZZA has some strengths to mitigate the risks. Although these aren't enough to drive growth, these aspects can help it protect its profitability and sustainability.

One aspect to consider is its business model. PZZA is geared more towards franchising than running owned restaurants. Its franchised restaurants are the main reason for its restaurant growth. And if you look back at its revenues, you can see that franchise royalties and fees still increased. This shows the resilience of this model. This should not be surprising because more franchised restaurants means more franchise revenues. The amount or percentage it gets from franchised restaurants is based on their sales and not net income. So, PZZA is sure to generate extra revenues. Additionally, this model is low-cost, unlike running owned restaurants because it does not have to worry about their day-to-day expenses. This allows it to expand without bleeding out or borrowing huge amount. That is why its operating cash inflow remains enough to cover capex and dividends. This is supported by its expansion in the international market. In fact, 99% of PZZA stores there are franchised with robust demand considering their systemwide sales growth there. My concern is that they are still not enough to completely offset the weakness in the North American segment.

Its liquidity may also act as its support to ensure longevity of its increasing capacity and dividends. Cash and equivalents are still stable at $37M, which is enough to cover the current portion of its borrowings. If I add accounts receivables, it can also cover accounts payable, lease liabilities, and deferred revenues. PZZA complements it with prudent debt management through timely issuances and repayments. With that, the maturity profile of borrowings remain long since 99% of the amount will not mature within a year. To be more specific, 95% has a maturity of about four years. This is still in line with the Net Debt/EBITDA of 3.4x, which means that it only needs 3.4 years theoretically to repay the whole amount even if it doesn't have to. Hence, you can prove that it still has enough buffer to sustain its operating capacity and dividends.

Valuation

To reassess its valuation, I decided to update its Dividend Discount Model. The annualized dividend is $1.84 based on the most recent quarterly payout of $0.46, which gives a yield of 5.16%. The dividend growth rate based on the three-year CAGR is 6.11%. Meanwhile, I set its risk premium at 5.5%. I realized that 5.0% is a bit low to be conservative. That way, we can price in more risks. Lastly, its Beta is 1.16, which means that the stock price of PZZA has become more volatile. This also shows that its price swings are wider than that of the S&P 500. With all this information, the TP or target price is $39.40, which suggests a 10% upside potential.

Technicals

PZZA's technicals are still weak today as the price remains close to its one-year low. The 50-day SMA is still in a downtrend, which means that recent market sentiments have weakened. On a lighter note, we can see recovery attempts. The recent price increase has broken above the 50-day SMA, which means that the current momentum is stronger than the average in the past 50 days. Also, the most recent low of $31.51 did not break below the previous low of $30.43. This means that buyers are starting to enter again and fewer sellers are accepting any price below $30.

Meanwhile, MACD and RSI show some improvements. The MACD line is increasing above the signal line, which means that buying volume is increasing as well. The histogram is back to positive territory, which implies that buying volume has outpaced selling volume recently. Lastly, RSI shows that the buying trend was due to overselling. At this point, the trend has normalized, so the uptrend may slow down. Nonetheless, it is still below the overbought region, so there is still enough buying volume. The only challenge is that their trend remains consistent with the price, so we cannot detect any bullish divergence that may lead to a breakout.

Conclusion

Papa John's International, Inc. remains resilient with its increasing international expansion to find more opportunities in less mature markets and lower its exposure to competition with DPZ. Its fundamentals and well-diversified business model also support its sustainability and dividend longevity. Meanwhile, valuation is justifiable with some upside potential, although not that much to entice buying surge. Technicals are still weak, but buying opportunities are present. I'm reiterating my buy rating, although I still advise caution amid macroeconomic volatility.
